planting.note: Plant divisions in spring as a low carpet at the front of a bed.

directions: Plant as a spreading mat in moist, well-drained soil in sun or light shade. Water while establishing and trim any winter-tattered foliage in early spring. Short pink pokers rise above the mat in summer and deepen to red as they fade, and the leaves turn russet in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a weed-smothering groundcover in moist soil.

directions: Set plants close together to knit into a dense mat over moist, well-drained soil. Keep watered until established and shear off old foliage before new spring growth. Pink flower spikes darken to deep red as summer passes, and the leaves color bronze-red into winter.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring as a low edging or groundcover.

directions: Grow in moist, well-drained soil in full sun for the most flowers and best leaf color. Water through establishment and tidy the mat in early spring. Salmon-pink pokers age to rich red through summer, backed by leaves that turn coppery in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a vigorous carpeting groundcover.

directions: Plant as a spreading mat in moist, well-drained soil in sun or dappled shade. Keep evenly watered while young and trim faded foliage in spring. Two-tone spikes open pale pink and deepen to red, with foliage turning bronze in the cool of autumn.

planting.note: Plant out after frost danger passes; grown mainly for its foliage.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained soil in sun to part shade, giving afternoon shade where summers are hot. Pinch stems to keep the clump bushy and cut back hard if it sprawls. The chevron-marked burgundy leaves are the main feature, joined by tiny white flowers in late summer.

planting.note: Plant after the last frost; valued for silver-marked foliage.

directions: Grow in moist, well-drained soil in part shade to keep the silvery leaf markings crisp. Pinch young shoots for density and shear back leggy stems in summer. The silver-and-burgundy foliage carries the display, with small white flower sprays in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a moist, part-shaded spot; grown for variegated leaves.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich soil in dappled shade to protect the variegation from scorch. Remove any all-green reverted shoots and cut back in late winter. The cream, green and chocolate-marked leaves are the draw, with thin sprays of tiny red flowers in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist soil in sun or part shade.

directions: Grow in moist, fertile soil in part shade and keep it watered in dry spells. Cut back in late winter and pull any self-sown seedlings you do not want. Green leaves marked with a dark maroon chevron are the highlight, with wiry sprays of small red flowers in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a moist woodland setting; it self-sows freely.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich soil in dappled shade where it naturalizes in a woodland garden. Deadhead if you want to limit its ready self-seeding and cut back in late winter. Thin spikes of tiny red-and-white flowers appear in late summer on this native woodlander.

planting.note: Plant in spring with plenty of room; a mature clump is shrub-sized.

directions: Give this large, non-running clump-former deep fertile soil and space to fill out. Water well in the first two seasons and cut the whole clump to the ground in late winter. Great plumes of creamy-white flowers rise on tall stems from early summer into autumn without spreading invasively.

planting.note: Plant after frost as a groundcover or trailer; can be an annual in cold zones.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in sun to light shade as a low trailing carpet or in containers. Trim to keep it in bounds since it roots as it spreads and can seed about in mild climates. Small round pink flower buttons appear over the bronze-tinted leaves for much of the warm season.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a spreading mat over rock or bank edges.

directions: Grow in moist, well-drained soil in sun or part shade where it can trail over rocks and walls. Water while establishing and trim wayward stems to shape the mat. Short rose-pink pokers stud the small glossy leaves in late summer and autumn as the foliage reddens.

planting.note: Plant after frost; grown for boldly marked foliage.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained soil in part shade to keep the leaf colors vivid. Pinch shoots for a fuller habit and cut back if stems flop in midsummer. The purple, green and silver patterned foliage is the main show, with airy white flower sprays late in the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring with space; forms a tall airy clump.

directions: Give this tall clump-former deep, moist, fertile soil and full sun to part shade. Water through the first seasons and cut stems to the ground in late winter. Loose sprays of creamy-white flowers top the tall stems through summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny bed with room around each clump.

directions: Space this mildew-resistant white selection generously and water at the soil line to keep foliage dry. Cut a third of the stems at the base in late spring for sturdier, better-ventilated growth. Pure white, fragrant clusters bloom for weeks in mid to late summer and draw butterflies.

planting.note: Set divisions out in spring in full sun and rich, moist soil.

directions: Plant in fertile soil and keep it consistently moist through the heat of summer. Thin crowded stems in spring to improve airflow and reduce mildew. Soft pink flowers marked with a crimson central eye open across mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; this vigorous selection can stand tall without staking.

directions: Give this tall, mildew-resistant cultivar full sun and steady moisture at the roots. Its strong stems rarely flop, but leave space between clumps for airflow. Vivid magenta-pink panicles bloom heavily from midsummer into early autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun where its tall stems have room to rise.

directions: This tall, exceptionally mildew-resistant selection thrives in sun with even moisture. Its small individual florets are clustered into dense heads that butterflies swarm. Lavender-pink flowers appear from midsummer into fall and rank among the best phlox for pollinators.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun for the deepest flower color.

directions: Set in rich, moisture-retentive soil and water at the base to keep leaves dry. Flowers shift with the light, reading violet-blue in cool morning air and warmer purple by midday. Fragrant clusters bloom from mid to late summer above dark stems.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and fertile, well-drained ground.

directions: Keep the soil evenly moist and thin the stems in spring to limit mildew. Each fuchsia-purple floret carries a small white star at its center. Bold, showy panicles bloom through mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; new foliage emerges bronze-red before turning green.

directions: Site in full sun with steady moisture and airy spacing between plants. Deep bronze-tinged spring foliage sets off the flowers to come. Intense cherry-red panicles open in mid to late summer, among the brightest reds in the group.

planting.note: Plant compact divisions in spring in a sunny border.

directions: This tidy, floriferous cultivar wants full sun and consistent moisture. Deadhead the first flush to encourage a second round of bloom. Clear rose-pink flowers with a red eye appear from mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in sun to light afternoon shade.

directions: Grow in fertile, evenly moist soil with good air circulation. Water low and mulch to steady soil temperature and moisture. Soft lilac flowers with a subtly paler eye bloom fragrantly through mid to late summer.

planting.note: Also sold as 'Duesterlohe'; plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Give this saturated selection full sun and rich, moist soil to fuel its color. Dark stems and buds add contrast before the flowers open. Glowing magenta-purple panicles bloom from mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun for warmest color.

directions: Site in full sun and keep the soil evenly moist during summer heat. Thin the stems in spring for airflow and cleaner foliage. Warm salmon-orange flowers, unusual among phlox, open from mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant this compact selection in spring at the front of a sunny bed.

directions: A dwarf, bushy phlox for the border edge or a large container, grown in full sun with even moisture. Keep foliage dry and spacing open to limit mildew. Pinwheel flowers striped in pink and white bloom through mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun and fertile soil.

directions: This mildew-resistant cultivar prefers sun and consistent moisture at the root. Water at the base and give clumps breathing room. Lavender-blue flowers centered with a crisp white eye bloom from mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; despite the name it grows quite tall.

directions: A tall, vigorous and mildew-resistant selection for the back of a sunny border. Keep it evenly watered and thin the stems in spring. Rich pink flowers with a darker eye bloom over a long mid to late summer season.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun where the dark foliage will show.

directions: Grow in full sun with steady moisture to support strong, mildew-resistant growth. Glossy dark green leaves make a striking backdrop for the blooms. Vibrant coral-pink flowers open from mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant this compact cultivar in spring near the front of a sunny bed.

directions: A short, well-branched phlox suited to border edges and containers in full sun. Keep moisture even and airflow open around the plant. Creamy flowers washed with coral bloom through mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun and fertile ground.

directions: This richly colored selection wants full sun and even soil moisture. Water at the base and thin stems in spring to keep foliage clean. Deep violet-purple flowers with a bright white eye bloom in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Also sold as 'Fujiyama'; plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Give this pure-white cultivar full sun, rich soil, and steady moisture. Its stems are strong and the bloom season runs late. Large, conical white panicles open from midsummer well into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; flower color shifts as blooms age.

directions: Grow in full sun with consistent moisture for the fullest color display. Unusual blooms blend cream, yellow, and pink and change tone as they mature. Multicolored clusters open through mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant this compact selection in spring at the front of the border.

directions: A dwarf, densely flowered phlox for edging and containers in full sun. Keep soil evenly moist and give plants airflow. Star-shaped rosy-purple flowers cover the plant through mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions or plugs in spring or early fall in full sun.

directions: Set plants into gritty, well-drained soil where the needle-like mats can spread over slopes and walls. Water until established, then keep on the dry side since soggy ground rots the crowns. Sheets of small flowers blanket the foliage in early to mid spring; shear lightly afterward to keep it dense.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun with excellent drainage.

directions: Plant into gritty soil where the low mat can knit together and spill over edges. Keep it dry between waterings and shear after flowering to renew the cushion. Bright pink flowers cover the plant in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun and gritty, drained soil.

directions: Grow on a sunny bank in lean soil, keeping it dry once the mat has taken hold. Shear after flowering to maintain a compact cushion. Vivid magenta-red flowers cover the plant in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun with sharp drainage.

directions: Plant into well-drained soil where the evergreen mat can spread as a groundcover. Water only until established and avoid winter wet. Purple flowers with a darker eye smother the foliage in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun and lean, drained soil.

directions: Set into gritty soil on a sunny slope and keep it on the dry side once rooted. Shear after flowering to keep the cushion dense. Crimson-red flowers marked with a deeper eye cover the mat in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun with excellent drainage.

directions: Grow in fast-draining soil in full sun, watering only to establish the mat. Trim lightly after bloom to renew the foliage. Pure white flowers blanket the plant in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun and gritty soil.

directions: Plant on a sunny bank in sharply drained soil, keeping it dry once settled. Shear back lightly after flowering to hold its shape. Clean white flowers cover the evergreen mat in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun with sharp drainage.

directions: Grow in lean, well-drained soil in full sun and water sparingly once rooted. Shear after bloom to keep the cushion compact. Rich rosy-red flowers smother the mat in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun and drained soil.

directions: This selection produces unusually large flowers on a dense cushion of foliage. Grow in gritty, well-drained soil and keep it dry once established. Big deep-pink blooms cover the plant in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun and fast-draining soil.

directions: A vigorous, fragrant selection that knits into a broad mat on sunny slopes. Water only to establish, then keep on the dry side. Deep rose-pink flowers cover the foliage in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun with sharp drainage.

directions: Grow in lean, well-drained soil in full sun and water sparingly once rooted. Shear lightly after flowering to keep it neat. White to pale pink flowers centered with a coral eye cover the mat in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun and gritty soil.

directions: Plant into fast-draining soil where the mat can spread as groundcover. Keep it dry between waterings and shear after bloom. White flowers accented with a rose-pink eye blanket the plant in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun with excellent drainage.

directions: Grow on a sunny slope or wall in sharply drained soil, watering only to establish. Trim after flowering to keep the cushion tight. Warm pink flowers cover the evergreen mat in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in part shade and moist, humus-rich soil.

directions: Site in dappled shade with consistently moist, organic soil for the woodland edge. Its broad, overlapping petals give an especially full flower. Deep blue-violet, fragrant blooms open in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in shade to part shade and moist soil.

directions: This low, spreading native roots along the ground to form a shade groundcover. Grow in moist, humus-rich soil under trees and keep it from drying out. Fragrant purple flowers rise on short stems in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun and very sharp drainage.

directions: A compact, cushion-forming alpine phlox for rock gardens and troughs in gritty soil. Give it full sun and keep it dry, especially over winter. Bright magenta-red flowers nearly hide the tiny mat in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after the ground has warmed and settled.

directions: Set divisions with the crown level with the soil and water them in well. This compact selection carries deep rosy-pink spikes on sturdy stems that rarely need staking. Bright flowers open late in the season, making a strong show when many perennials fade; cut back after bloom to keep the patch neat.

planting.note: Divide and replant in spring while growth is young.

directions: Plant divisions in fertile, moisture-retentive soil and mulch to hold water. Pure white flower spikes rise earlier than many pink types and light up a partly shaded border. Pinch stems once in early summer for a bushier plant, and enjoy the clean white bloom from midsummer onward.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring in a spot with reliable moisture.

directions: Space divisions generously so the upright spikes have room to stand tall. Deep rose-purple flowers crowd the tips of strong stems from late summer into autumn. Keep the soil damp during dry spells and stake in exposed sites; cut stalks for the vase as buds begin to color.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; this clumping form stays put and needs no barrier.

directions: Set the crown at soil level and water in a well-behaved clump that does not run like the wild species. Snowy white spikes appear from early summer and repeat if spent stems are trimmed. This tidy selection suits smaller beds where the spreading types would overwhelm neighbors.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into moist, fertile ground.

directions: Like its white counterpart, this selection forms a well-mannered clump instead of a running colony. Soft pink flower spikes open through mid and late summer above dark green foliage. Keep it watered in heat and shear lightly after the first flush to encourage more spikes.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring and replant promptly.

directions: Give this tall selection deep, moist soil and full sun for the strongest stems. Rich rosy-pink spikes make long-lasting cut flowers and draw bees and butterflies. Support the stems in windy sites and divide every few years to keep the colony vigorous.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in consistently moist soil.

directions: Set divisions where they get even moisture and morning sun. Crisp white flower spikes crown the stems from late summer into fall, brightening the back of a border. Deadhead finished spikes and cut the plant to the ground after frost blackens the foliage.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter for bloom the first season.

directions: Press the seed onto the surface and give light and warmth to germinate within a few weeks. This dwarf, first-year-flowering strain blooms in white the same season it is sown, making it useful as a quick perennial or container filler. Keep it evenly watered and it will bloom for weeks from summer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, average soil.

directions: This classic rose-pink form spreads freely by rhizomes, so plant it where it can roam or contain the roots. Tall flower spikes open from the bottom up in late summer and last well in bouquets. Water during drought and lift and divide clumps every two or three years to renew them.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring in a moisture-holding bed.

directions: The white-flowered form of the species performs like the wild plant and spreads by underground runners. Plant it in damp ground where its late spikes brighten a cottage border. Give steady moisture through summer and shear off faded spikes to keep the bed looking fresh.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; propagate only by division to keep the variegation.

directions: Grown as much for its cream-edged leaves as its pink flowers, this form lights up a border even before bloom. Plant divisions in moist soil and give a little afternoon shade in hot regions to protect the variegation. Rosy spikes appear in late summer above the marbled foliage.

planting.note: Sow seed indoors or set divisions in spring in a damp, sunny spot.

directions: This southern native prefers wet meadows and pond edges, so plant it where soil stays reliably moist. Slender stems carry narrow leaves and pink to lavender flowers earlier in the season than common obedient plant. Give it full sun and room to naturalize; blooms open in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter or plant into consistently wet ground.

directions: A wetland species of the south-central states, this slender obedient plant thrives in ditches, marsh edges, and rain gardens. Sow the fine seed on the surface and keep it wet until sprouts show. Airy spikes of pale pink flowers rise on thin stems in late spring and summer.

planting.note: Sow indoors or set out young plants in spring in moist soil.

directions: This tall Gulf Coast native can top five feet, so place it at the back of a moist border. Broad clasping leaves climb sturdy stems topped with pale pink to white flowers. Keep the ground damp and give full sun; the large spikes open in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter or plant into a wet, sunny site.

directions: Native to streambanks and wet meadows across the northern and western states, this small-flowered species suits a bog or pond edge. Its shorter spikes carry closely set pink-purple flowers. Sow the seed on moist mix and keep it cool; give the plant wet feet and full sun for best growth.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter or plant divisions out in spring once frost danger passes.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it lightly moist and warm until germination. This compact selection holds its deep violet-blue bells on sturdy stems that rarely need staking. Watch for inflated buds in early summer that pop open into wide five-pointed stars; cut back after flowering to keep plants tidy.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter; this series often blooms the first year from an early start.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto the surface and provide bright light and steady warmth for even sprouting. This tall cut-flower type carries large blue balloon buds on long straight stems ideal for the vase. Harvest stems as the first buds crack open, and expect blooms in the first season from an early indoor start.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for possible first-year bloom, or set plants out after frost.

directions: Sow on the surface and keep just moist until seedlings emerge, then grow on in bright light. Soft blush-pink balloons open to pale veined stars atop tall stems that suit cutting. Give well-drained soil and let the deep root settle undisturbed; blooms arrive in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter or plant out young stock after the last frost.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and hold the tray warm and evenly damp until germination. Crisp white balloon buds swell on tall upright stems, opening into clean starry bells that read well from a distance. Plant in fertile, well-drained ground and stake only if grown in shade; flowers come through summer.

planting.note: Sow indoors early for first-year bloom on this dwarf edging type.

directions: Surface-sow and keep lightly moist under bright light until the seedlings stand up. This low, mounding dwarf covers itself in deep blue-purple stars and works well at the front of a bed or in a pot. Pinch off faded flowers to keep new buds forming; expect bloom the first summer from an early start.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter; the Astra series flowers reliably in its first year.

directions: Press the seed onto the surface and give it light and warmth to sprout evenly. This tidy dwarf produces unusually full double blue flowers on compact plants suited to containers and edging. Keep the soil well drained and remove spent blooms; the first flowers open in early summer from an early sowing.

planting.note: Sow indoors early for compact first-year plants, or set out after frost.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep the mix damp and bright until germination. Soft pink double stars top this dwarf, front-of-border plant that also grows well in pots. Give it sun and sharp drainage, deadhead often, and enjoy blooms from early summer into fall.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter for bloom the same season.

directions: Surface-sow and hold under bright light at room warmth until seedlings emerge. Pure white double flowers cover this compact dwarf, brightening the edge of a path or a mixed container. Plant in well-drained soil, pinch off spent blooms, and expect the first stars in early summer.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter for first-year flowering on this dwarf type.

directions: Press the fine seed onto the surface and keep it lit and lightly moist until it sprouts. Semi-double blue-violet flowers with an extra ring of petals cover this neat, low-growing plant. Grow in full sun with good drainage and remove faded blooms to extend the display through summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter or plant out in spring after frost.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it evenly damp and warm until germination. This selection bears fully double blue flowers layered with many petals for a fuller look than the single species. Give it well-drained soil and steady moisture; the puffy buds open through mid to late summer.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter or set young plants out once frost has passed.

directions: Surface-sow and keep lightly moist under bright conditions until seedlings appear. Double white flowers with layered petals give this plant a peony-like fullness in the border. Plant in fertile, well-drained soil, leave the taproot undisturbed, and enjoy blooms across summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter or plant divisions in spring after frost.

directions: Press the seed onto the surface and keep it warm and just moist until it germinates. This novelty holds its blue-violet flowers in the puffed balloon stage, so the buds stay inflated and never fully open. Grow in sun with good drainage and cut a few stems for a curious, long-lasting display.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter or plant out in spring; ideal for rock gardens.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed and keep it lit and lightly damp until seedlings stand. This alpine dwarf forms a low mat topped by large violet-blue stars almost as big as the plant. Give it gritty, sharply drained soil in a trough or rock garden; flowers open through summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter or plant out after the last spring frost.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep the tray moist and warm until germination. This compact form carries rich blue single bells on short, self-supporting stems that seldom flop. Plant in well-drained soil and deadhead through the season; inflated buds open from early to late summer.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter or set plants out once frost danger passes.

directions: Surface-sow and keep lightly moist under bright light until seedlings emerge. This dwarf white form holds clean star-shaped bells on sturdy, compact stems. Give it sun and good drainage, leave the deep root undisturbed, and remove faded flowers to keep blooms coming all summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter or plant out in spring after the last frost.

directions: Press the seed onto the surface and keep it warm and just damp until germination. This form bears layered double blue-violet flowers for a fuller look than the single species. Grow in fertile, well-drained soil, deadhead regularly, and expect blooms from mid to late summer.

planting.note: Sow indoors early for compact first-year plants.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it lit and lightly moist until seedlings rise. This dwarf, well-branched selection covers itself in blue-violet stars on a compact, container-friendly frame. Give it full sun and sharp drainage, pinch off spent flowers, and enjoy bloom from early summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for bloom the first season.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and keep the mix damp under bright light until germination. This compact type produces soft pink balloon buds that open to veined starry bells on tidy plants. Plant in sun with good drainage, remove faded blooms, and expect flowers from early to late summer.

planting.note: Sow indoors early for compact first-year flowering plants.

directions: Surface-sow and hold the tray warm and evenly moist under light until seedlings emerge. Crisp white balloon buds open to clean stars on this compact, front-of-border selection. Give it full sun and free-draining soil, deadhead often, and enjoy blooms through the summer.

planting.note: Cold-moist stratify seed for a few weeks, then start indoors or sow in a shaded outdoor bed.

directions: Barely cover seed and keep the flat cool and damp until sprouts appear. This eastern North American native forms low mounds in woodland soil and spreads slowly into a loose groundcover. Airy sky-blue flowers open in mid to late spring, a favorite of early bees.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter; this short-lived species often blooms its first summer.

directions: Surface-sow and keep warm and moist for even sprouting. Unlike its blue relatives it carries pendant, tubular pale-yellow trumpets flushed with red on the outside. Blooms through summer and self-sows gently to persist where it is short-lived; deadhead if you want to limit seedlings.

planting.note: Give seed a cold-moist period, as this alpine species germinates best after winter chill.

directions: Sow in gritty, free-draining soil and stratify cold before warmth to break dormancy. This compact western mountain plant forms neat rosettes topped by clusters of blue flowers with yellow throats. Ideal for rock gardens and troughs; flowers appear in late spring atop short stems.

planting.note: Start seed indoors after a short chill; set out sturdy plants once frost danger passes.

directions: Barely cover seed and keep the mix evenly moist until sprouts stand up. This is one of the taller, longer-lived species, forming leafy clumps topped with violet-blue flowers. It blooms across early to midsummer and appreciates rich soil that never dries out completely.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed, then start indoors; this Pacific species enjoys cool springs.

directions: Sow onto a moist surface and chill before warmth to prompt sprouting. Prized for large flowers that open in soft shades of pink, salmon, and lavender that deepen as they age. Give it dappled shade and steady moisture; bloom comes in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter and transplant clumps out after the last frost.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and keep it cool and damp while it sprouts. This Japanese species has finely divided foliage and rich blue flower clusters in late spring. It is the parent of several dark-leaved garden forms and thrives in humus-rich, moisture-retentive soil.

planting.note: This arctic species needs a cold-moist spell before warmth to sprout reliably.

directions: Sow into gritty soil and stratify cold to mimic its far-northern winters. It forms a tidy low tuft topped with clear blue flowers on short stems in late spring. Extremely cold-hardy but resents heat and humidity, so give it a cool spot with good drainage.

planting.note: An alpine specialist that needs prolonged cold stratification and sharp drainage.

directions: Sow in a very gritty mix and give a long cold-moist period, as high-alpine seed can be slow and erratic. The sticky, ferny foliage carries a skunky scent, while the flowers are deep blue with contrasting orange stamens. Best in a trough or scree bed with full sun and lean, fast-draining soil.

planting.note: Chill seed briefly, then start indoors; this species likes consistently moist ground.

directions: Barely cover seed and keep it cool and wet until it sprouts. A tall streamside species that wants damp to boggy soil and does not tolerate drought. It sends up leafy stems tipped with blue flower clusters in early summer, excellent along pond edges or in rain gardens.

planting.note: A boreal species that germinates best after a cold-moist stratification.

directions: Sow and chill cold before warmth to satisfy its northern dormancy. This circumpolar plant carries nodding blue-violet bells on slender stems in early summer. Very cold-tolerant, it prefers cool, moist meadow soil and struggles in hot southern gardens.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring; this variegated form does not come true from seed.

directions: Plant divisions at the same depth they grew, in cool, moisture-retentive soil with some afternoon shade. Each leaflet is crisply edged in cream, so grow it in bright shade to keep the variegation clean and avoid leaf scorch. Lavender-blue flowers rise above the striped foliage in early summer, though the leaves are the main attraction.

planting.note: Divide and replant in spring; propagate by division to keep the variegated leaves.

directions: Plant in humus-rich woodland soil and keep it evenly moist in dappled shade. The white-edged leaves take on a rosy pink flush in cool spring weather before settling to green and cream. Soft blue flowers appear in late spring above the mounded, colorful foliage.

planting.note: Increase by division in spring, since this variegated selection will not seed true.

directions: Set divisions into rich, moist soil in a lightly shaded spot. An improved white-margined form with slender, refined leaflets and reddish stems that hold their color well. Nodding sky-blue bells open in late spring above the crisp, mounding foliage.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; this dark-leaved selection is propagated vegetatively.

directions: Set plants in fertile, moist soil where morning sun brings out the leaf color. New growth emerges deep purple-black and matures to bronze-green, a striking foil for the flowers. Clusters of clear blue bells open in late spring against the dark, ferny leaves.

planting.note: Grown from seed or division; seedlings show varying degrees of purple leaf tint.

directions: Surface-sow and keep cool and moist, or divide established clumps in spring. Foliage flushes deep purple-bronze in spring, most intense in cooler weather and brighter light. Tall stems carry violet-blue flowers in early summer above the dusky leaves.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter; the white flower color usually comes fairly true.

directions: Barely cover seed and keep the flat cool and damp until it sprouts. The pure white form of the classic species, its ladder-like leaves topped by clusters of nodding white bells. Blooms in late spring; shear back after flowering to keep the clump neat.

planting.note: Start seed early indoors; this compact selection can bloom its first season.

directions: Barely cover seed and grow on cool until you can set out young plants. A dwarf, uniform creeping form that stays neat and floriferous in the front of a shade bed. Bright blue flowers cover the low mound in mid to late spring.

planting.note: This sterile hybrid is increased by division rather than seed.

directions: Plant divisions in rich, moist soil in sun to light shade. Purple-tinged young foliage sets off masses of fragrant lavender-blue flowers with darker veining. Because it sets little seed it flowers over a long stretch from late spring into summer; trim spent stems to keep it fresh.

planting.note: Increase this selection by spring division to keep it uniform.

directions: Set divisions into fertile, evenly moist soil in partial shade. A vigorous selection valued for larger, well-formed flowers held in full clusters. Clear blue blooms with pale centers open in early summer over tidy green foliage.

planting.note: Start seed early indoors; this compact form often blooms in its first year.

directions: Surface-sow and keep cool and moist until sprouts appear. A dense, dwarf selection that stays compact and floriferous even in containers. Large blue flowers with bright golden eyes cover the low mound from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Sow early indoors; the golden foliage color comes fairly true from seed.

directions: Barely cover seed and grow on in bright, cool conditions. The ferny foliage emerges golden-yellow in spring, glowing brightest in a spot with morning sun and afternoon shade. Pale blue flowers rise above the bright leaves in early summer.

planting.note: A variegated form that must be divided rather than grown from seed.

directions: Plant divisions in cool, humus-rich soil in bright shade to protect the leaf edges. Each leaflet carries a wide white margin, and the plant is topped by deep sapphire-blue flowers. Bloom comes in early summer, but the boldly edged foliage carries the display all season.

planting.note: Start early indoors; this dwarf strain reliably flowers its first season.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and keep it cool and damp until sprouts stand up. A compact, uniform selection bred for pots and the front of the border, it stays tidy and blooms early. Clusters of bright blue bells appear from late spring, often within the first year from an early sowing.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed before warmth; also propagated by division to hold its color.

directions: Sow onto moist soil and give a cold spell before warmth to prompt sprouting. Selected for large flowers in warm apricot and soft pink tones that deepen with age. Give it dappled shade and steady moisture, and expect bloom in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: Plant dormant rhizomes in autumn or early spring in humus-rich woodland soil.

directions: Set rhizomes horizontally about two inches deep with the growth buds pointing upward and slightly toward the surface. Mulch with leaf litter and keep the soil evenly moist through the first summer while roots establish. Arching stems hang paired greenish-white bells beneath the foliage in late spring, followed by blue-black berries.

planting.note: Plant in cool woodland shade; naturalizes into broad colonies over time.

directions: Set rhizomes horizontally about two inches down in humus-rich, moist soil beneath trees or shrubs. Allow the clump to spread undisturbed and topdress with leaf mold each autumn. Clusters of small green-tipped white bells dangle from arching stems in late spring, giving way to blue-black fruit.

planting.note: This vigorous hybrid is best increased by division of the rhizomes.

directions: Plant thick rhizomes two to three inches deep in fertile, moisture-holding shade soil. This cross of P. multiflorum and P. odoratum is robust and quick to fill a bed, so give it room and mulch generously. Elegant white bells line the tall arching stems in late spring and are a favorite for cutting.

planting.note: A low groundcover form; plant in cool shade near the front of the bed.

directions: Plant shallow rhizomes an inch deep in a shaded pocket with rich, evenly moist soil. This diminutive species creeps to form a tidy carpet, making it ideal for shade edging or a woodland trough. Short upright stems carry single white bells in late spring above neat green leaves.

planting.note: Plant slender rhizomes in dappled shade with steady moisture.

directions: Set the fine rhizomes an inch or two below the surface in a cool, leafy soil. The narrow sickle-shaped leaves give this species a graceful arching outline in shade plantings. Small white bells hang beneath the stems in late spring, followed by dark berries.

planting.note: Plant in cool, moist upland shade; leaves are arranged in whorls up the stem.

directions: Plant rhizomes two inches deep in cool, humus-rich soil that never fully dries. The narrow leaves ring the upright stems in distinct whorls, unlike the alternate-leaved species. Small greenish-white bells cluster in the leaf joints in early summer, ripening to red berries.

planting.note: A tiny alpine species; plant in gritty, humus-rich soil in cool shade.

directions: Plant the small rhizomes just below the surface in a sharply drained yet moisture-retentive gritty mix, ideal for a trough or rock garden. This mat-forming miniature hugs the ground and dislikes hot, dry conditions. Comparatively large upward-facing lilac-pink flowers open almost at soil level in late spring.

planting.note: An eastern North American native for moist woodland shade.

directions: Plant rhizomes about two inches deep in rich, moist forest-floor soil under a leafy canopy. This native is told from smooth Solomon's seal by the fine hairs on the leaf undersides. Pairs of small yellow-green bells hang from arching stems in late spring, followed by dark blue berries.

planting.note: A Himalayan species with tendril-tipped leaves; give it cool moist shade.

directions: Plant rhizomes two inches deep in a cool, humus-laden woodland soil that stays evenly damp. The whorled leaves end in curling tendrils that let the tall stems lean on nearby plants. Small pinkish to white bells appear in early summer among the upper leaves.

planting.note: A large southern Chinese species; plant in warm, sheltered shade.

directions: Plant the fat rhizomes two to three inches deep in rich, well-drained soil in a mild, shaded spot. This tall species has tendril-tipped whorled leaves and can lean or scramble, so a light support helps. Clusters of coral to orange-red bells hang along the stems in early summer.

planting.note: A robust East Asian species for rich, moist shade.

directions: Plant the knobby rhizomes about two inches deep in fertile, moisture-retentive soil under light shade. This species forms sturdy arching stems with broad leaves and thickens steadily once settled. Clusters of greenish-white bells hang from the stems in late spring, ripening to dark berries.

planting.note: A very hardy East Asian species with tendril-tipped whorled leaves.

directions: Plant rhizomes two inches deep in cool, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade. The narrow whorled leaves end in fine curling tips that let the stems clasp support. Small white bells hang in the leaf axils in late spring, followed by black berries.

planting.note: A compact East Asian species noted for leafy bracts under the flowers.

directions: Plant rhizomes an inch or two deep in a cool, moist woodland soil rich in leaf mold. A pair of conspicuous green bracts sits just below each flower cluster, a distinctive touch for this species. Paired greenish-white bells hang beneath the arching stems in late spring.

planting.note: A Chinese species with pink bells and tendril-tipped leaves for cool shade.

directions: Plant rhizomes about two inches deep in cool, humus-rich soil that stays reliably moist. The slender stems bear whorled, tendril-tipped leaves and a graceful arching habit. Rose-pink to lilac bells hang among the upper leaves in early summer, ripening to red fruit.

planting.note: A tall scrambling species; give it rich moist shade and a little support.

directions: Plant rhizomes two to three inches deep in fertile, moisture-retentive shade soil. The whorled leaves end in curling tendrils, and the tall stems will lean on shrubs or a discreet stake. Dense clusters of pink-flushed bells hang from the upper stem in early summer.

planting.note: A modest species for cool partial shade and humus-rich soil.

directions: Plant rhizomes an inch or two deep in moist, leafy soil in dappled shade. This compact species suits the middle of a shade border or a woodland pocket. Small greenish-white bells hang beneath the arching stems in late spring.

planting.note: Increase this dark-shooted selection only by division of the rhizomes.

directions: Plant division rhizomes two inches deep in rich, moisture-holding shade soil. The chocolate-purple emerging shoots are the highlight, so place it where spring light shows off the new growth before it greens up. White bells follow along the arching stems in late spring.

planting.note: A vigorous, free-flowering selection best propagated by division.

directions: Plant rhizomes two to three inches deep in fertile, evenly moist shade soil. This selection is prized for strong stems and generous flowering, filling a bed quickly once established. Long rows of white bells hang beneath the tall arching stems in late spring.

planting.note: A compact hybrid for the shade-garden front; divide to propagate.

directions: Plant division rhizomes an inch or two deep in cool, humus-rich soil near the front of a shaded bed. This tidy dwarf hybrid stays low and neat, making it a good choice for edging or containers. Dangling white bells with green tips appear along the short stems in late spring.

planting.note: A cream-striped selection; divide to keep the variegation true.

directions: Plant division rhizomes an inch or two deep in rich, moisture-retentive shade soil. The boldly cream-striped leaves light up shady beds, so keep it out of hot direct sun that can scorch the pale tissue. Fragrant white bells hang from the arching stems in late spring.

planting.note: A Japanese selection with white-flushed new growth; divide to propagate.

directions: Plant division rhizomes an inch or two deep in cool, humus-rich shade soil. The upper leaves emerge brushed with white in spring, fading to green as the season warms, so early-season light shows it best. Fragrant white bells hang beneath the arching stems in late spring.

planting.note: A cream-margined form of the common species; divide to keep it true.

directions: Plant division rhizomes about two inches deep in moist, humus-rich shade soil. The pale-edged leaves brighten a dim corner, and the clump slowly widens into a graceful stand. White green-tipped bells dangle from the arching stems in late spring.

planting.note: A many-flowered variegated form; propagate by division of the rhizome.

directions: Plant division rhizomes an inch or two deep in cool, leaf-mold-rich shade soil. This form bears more flowers per node than the type and shows the same clean cream leaf edging. Clusters of fragrant white bells hang along the arching stems in late spring.

planting.note: A slender white-edged form; divide to maintain the variegation.

directions: Plant the fine rhizomes an inch deep in a cool, moisture-retentive shade soil. The narrow sickle-shaped leaves carry crisp white margins, giving a delicate look near the front of a shady bed. Small white bells hang beneath the arching stems in late spring.

planting.note: A red-flowered whorled selection for cool, moist shade; divide to propagate.

directions: Plant division rhizomes two inches deep in cool, humus-rich soil that stays evenly moist. The narrow leaves ring the upright stems in whorls, and this form adds warm reddish tones to the flowers and fruit. Small bells cluster in the leaf joints in early summer, ripening to red berries.

planting.note: Surface-sow indoors in late winter and give the seed a cold spell to break dormancy.

directions: Press the fine seed onto the surface of a moist mix and leave it uncovered, since light aids germination. Keep the pots cool and shaded until seedlings appear, then grow on in dappled shade with steady moisture. Set plants out in humus-rich soil and expect the pale yellow flowers to open in the earliest days of spring.

planting.note: Divide clumps or take root cuttings in early spring to keep the pure white form.

directions: Lift and split established crowns in spring, replanting the divisions in moist, humus-rich ground. Keep the soil reliably damp and shelter plants from hot afternoon sun. Snowball-white globes sit atop upright stems in early spring, holding their color as the leaves expand.

planting.note: Divide crowns after flowering to hold the deep crimson color true.

directions: Set divisions into constantly moist, rich soil at a pond edge or damp border. Water freely and mulch to keep the crowns cool through summer. Whorls of intense crimson-magenta flowers climb the stems in successive tiers from late spring into early summer.

planting.note: Propagate by division to keep the white flowers with their pink eye.

directions: Plant divisions in wet, humus-laden soil in dappled light and never allow them to parch. Feed lightly in spring and keep the ground damp all summer. Candelabra tiers of white flowers, each with a small rosy eye, open over a long stretch from late spring.

planting.note: Cool-sow and grow in boggy ground; this is the largest of the primroses.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed on a wet mix and keep it cool and bright until it germinates. Grow the plants in permanently moist, rich soil beside water where their roots can run cool. Tall stems carry large drooping heads of fragrant sulphur-yellow bells in mid to late summer, dusted with white farina.

planting.note: Sow cool; the plant goes summer-dormant and reappears each spring.

directions: Surface-sow and keep the pot cool until seedlings emerge, then grow them in leafy, moist woodland soil. Let the spreading rhizomes creep and expect the plant to die back and rest by midsummer. Delicate, often fringed flowers in white, pink, or lilac float above soft crinkled leaves in late spring.

planting.note: Cool-sow and plant in damp ground for the tiered candelabra bloom.

directions: Surface-sow onto a wet mix and hold it cool until germination, then grow the seedlings without letting them dry. Plant in constantly moist, rich soil in light shade. Whorls of orange buds open to deep golden-yellow flowers in successive tiers up the stem through early summer.

planting.note: Sow cool and grow bog-side; hybridizes freely with other candelabras.

directions: Press seed onto a moist surface and keep it cool until seedlings appear. Set plants in wet, humus-rich soil beside a stream or pond where they can naturalize. Tiers of rose-pink flowers with yellow eyes ring the tall stems in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Cool-sow; the flower stems are coated in white meal.

directions: Surface-sow onto wet compost and chill briefly, then grow seedlings on in cool, moist conditions. Plant in deep, rich, permanently damp soil in dappled shade. Whorls of deep red flowers with darker eyes climb white-mealy stems from late spring into summer.

planting.note: A seed strain in soft pink shades; sow cool for even germination.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it cool and moist until it sprouts, then grow the young plants without checking their growth. Plant in wet, humus-rich ground in light shade, ideally near water. Candelabra tiers in pastel pink to salmon shades open on mealy stems through early summer.

planting.note: Sow cool and grow in wet ground; blooms almost before the leaves appear.

directions: Surface-sow onto a saturated mix and keep it cool until germination. Plant in boggy, humus-rich soil that never dries out. Vivid rose-pink flowers open on short stems very early in spring, ahead of the young bronze-tinted foliage.

planting.note: Cool-sow this distinctive spiked species; it can be short-lived.

directions: Surface-sow onto a moist mix and keep it cool and bright until seedlings appear. Grow in reliably damp, well-drained soil in sun or light shade and allow it to self-sow to persist. Narrow poker-like spikes show red buds opening upward to lavender-blue flowers in early summer.

planting.note: Sow cool; needs sharp drainage as a crevice or trough alpine.

directions: Surface-sow onto a gritty, free-draining mix and keep it cool while germination proceeds. Grow the woody-based rosettes in a trough or rock crevice with excellent drainage. Powdery, toothed leaves edged in white meal set off clusters of lavender-blue flowers in early spring.

planting.note: Increase this old clone from cuttings or offsets to keep it true.

directions: Root short shoots or divide offsets in spring and grow on in very gritty, fast-draining soil. Site the rosettes in a trough or raised bed where winter wet drains away quickly. Broad heads of soft mauve-blue flowers open above silver-dusted, toothed foliage in early spring.

planting.note: Cool-sow; often short-lived, so let it self-seed to renew.

directions: Surface-sow onto a damp mix and hold it cool until seedlings emerge. Grow in moist, humus-rich soil in light shade and allow seedlings to appear for continuity. Flattened, drumstick-like heads of deep violet-blue flowers, mealy underneath, open in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Usually grown as a cool-season pot plant or winter annual from summer sowing.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed onto a fine, moist mix and keep it lightly warm and bright to germinate. Grow the plants cool and frost-free, in pots or as a winter bedder in mild regions. Airy tiered whorls of small lilac, pink, or white flowers rise above soft foliage in winter and early spring.

planting.note: Grown as a frost-free winter pot plant; foliage can irritate sensitive skin.

directions: Surface-sow onto a fine mix and keep it warm and evenly moist until seedlings appear. Grow cool and bright indoors or under glass, wearing gloves when handling the hairy leaves. Large rounded flowers in pink, lilac, red, or white are carried above the foliage over a long winter-to-spring season.

planting.note: Sow in a cool, bright spot; widely grown for spring bedding and pots.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it uncovered, cool, and moist to prompt germination. Grow the young plants in rich, moisture-retentive soil in dappled shade. Sturdy stems carry rounded heads of flat, often bright-eyed flowers in nearly every color from late winter through spring.

planting.note: An old show strain; sow cool to raise the gold-edged mahogany flowers.

directions: Surface-sow and keep the seed cool and uncovered until seedlings appear, then grow on steadily. Plant in fertile, moist soil in light shade and protect the neat rosettes from slugs. Each dark mahogany-red petal is finely rimmed and split with bright gold, forming tight heads in mid spring.

planting.note: A strain notable for eyeless flowers and dark foliage; sow cool.

directions: Surface-sow onto a moist mix and keep it cool and bright until germination. Grow the seedlings in rich, damp soil in partial shade. Velvety self-colored flowers without a contrasting eye, in wine, blue, and amber shades, sit above bronze-flushed leaves in spring.

planting.note: A vigorous old juliae hybrid; divide clumps to keep the deep color.

directions: Lift and split the spreading mats in spring or after flowering and replant into moist, fertile soil. Grow in dappled shade and keep the ground from drying out in summer. Rich claret-purple flowers with a small yellow eye smother the low bronze-green rosettes very early in spring.

planting.note: An old Garryarde selection with bronze foliage; increase by division.

directions: Divide the crowns in spring and set them into cool, humus-rich soil in light shade. Keep the plants evenly moist through the growing season. Soft lilac-pink flowers with a yellow eye rise on dark stems above deep bronze-purple leaves in early spring.

planting.note: A sterile double primrose; propagate only by division.

directions: Divide congested clumps every couple of years in spring and replant into rich, moist soil. Grow in partial shade and never let the roots dry out. Fully double, rosette-like flowers of deep violet-blue tipped with white open low among the leaves in spring.

planting.note: A double white primrose backed by a leafy green ruff; divide to propagate.

directions: Split the crowns after flowering and replant into cool, fertile, moisture-holding soil. Grow in dappled shade with steady watering. Fully double white flowers, each set off by a small green leafy collar, appear over the rosettes in spring.

planting.note: An unusual green-flowered clone; increase only by division.

directions: Divide the clumps in spring or early autumn and replant into rich, reliably moist soil. Grow in partial shade and keep well watered through summer. Ruffled, frilled flowers of soft green with a yellow center are carried in loose heads over a long spring season.

planting.note: An old Irish primrose; divide clumps to keep it going.

directions: Lift and divide the crowns every year or two in spring and replant into fresh, moist soil. Grow in light shade and feed lightly to keep the plants vigorous. Rose-pink flowers each striped down the center with a cream band form neat heads in early spring.

planting.note: A modern double primrose in the Belarina series; propagate by division.

directions: Divide the clumps every few years in spring and set them into rich, moisture-retentive soil. Grow in partial shade and deadhead to prolong the display. Fully double, rose-form flowers of soft creamy yellow bloom generously over a long spring season.

planting.note: A deep-red double in the Belarina series; increase by division.

directions: Split congested plants in spring and replant into fertile, moist soil in dappled shade. Keep watered and remove spent blooms to encourage more. Rich burgundy-red, fully double rosette flowers cover the compact clumps through spring.

planting.note: The naturally pink-flowered form of the wild primrose; sow cool.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and leave it uncovered in a cool, bright spot until it germinates. Grow the young plants in humus-rich, moisture-holding soil in light shade. Solitary pink to rose-lilac flowers with a yellow eye rise just above the fresh rosettes in very early spring.

planting.note: Cool-sow this moisture-loving species for damp, cool borders.

directions: Surface-sow onto a wet mix and keep it cool until seedlings emerge. Plant in rich, permanently moist soil in light shade or beside water. Loose one-sided heads of nodding funnel-shaped flowers in deep rose-red hang from slender mealy stems in early summer.

planting.note: A woodland species that goes dormant in summer; sow cool.

directions: Surface-sow and keep the pot cool until seedlings appear, then grow on in leafy, moisture-retentive soil. Plant in dappled woodland shade and expect the foliage to rest by midsummer. Airy heads of rose-pink flowers hover on slender stems above softly hairy, scalloped leaves in late spring.

planting.note: Cool-sow this fragrant species for moist, cool positions.

directions: Surface-sow onto a moist mix and keep it cool and bright until germination. Grow in deep, rich, reliably damp soil in light shade where the roots stay cool. Drooping heads of fragrant bell-shaped flowers, dusted with white meal, open in shades of yellow, cream, or violet in early summer.

planting.note: A candelabra species for bog gardens; sow cool and grow wet.

directions: Surface-sow onto saturated compost and keep it cool until seedlings appear. Plant in wet, humus-rich soil beside water where it can naturalize and hybridize. Whorls of pale orange to apricot flowers ring the tall stems in successive tiers in early summer.

planting.note: A small farinose species; sow cool and grow in gritty, moist soil.

directions: Surface-sow onto a moist, gritty mix and keep it cool until germination. Grow in a raised bed or trough with good drainage but steady moisture. Compact heads of pink to lilac yellow-eyed flowers rise on short mealy stems above white-dusted rosettes in mid spring.

planting.note: Set plants in humus-rich shade in early spring or divide after flowering.

directions: Plant crowns at the same depth they grew, spacing them to allow the leaves to knit into groundcover. Keep the soil evenly moist and mulch with leaf mold, cutting back tired foliage after bloom to encourage fresh silver-spotted leaves. Flowers open pink and age to blue in early spring, often before the foliage fully expands.

planting.note: Plant in dappled shade with moist, rich soil in early spring.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil and water deeply until established. The heavily silver-spotted leaves resist mildew best in cool, moist shade, so avoid hot afternoon sun and dry soil. Funnel-shaped flowers shift from reddish-pink to violet-blue in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in shade in early spring; one of the earliest lungworts to bloom.

directions: Plant crowns in rich, moist woodland soil and keep them from drying out. The plain soft-green leaves lack silver spotting, but the coral-red flowers are among the first to open, sometimes in late winter in mild areas. Shear spent flower stems to tidy the vigorous, spreading clump.

planting.note: Plant in cool, moist shade in early spring.

directions: Give this larger species room, planting in deep, moist, humus-rich soil. The velvety, unspotted green leaves are softly hairy and can grow large where moisture is steady. Clusters of flowers open reddish-violet and turn blue in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in shade in early spring; divide after flowering to keep vigor.

directions: Plant this named selection in moist, shaded soil and mulch to conserve moisture. Buds open pale pink and mature to pure white flowers over silver-spotted leaves in early spring, brightening dim corners. Cut back after bloom to refresh the foliage.

planting.note: Plant in dappled shade in early spring; a long-cultivated classic.

directions: Set plants in rich, moist soil and keep them out of hot sun to limit powdery mildew. The broad leaves are boldly spotted with silver, and the flowers pass from rose-pink buds to blue in early spring. Divide crowded clumps every few years to maintain healthy foliage.

planting.note: Plant in partial to full shade in early spring.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained soil rich in organic matter and mulch lightly. Upright silver-spotted leaves form a tidy mound topped by coral-pink to raspberry flowers in mid spring. It shows good mildew resistance when kept evenly watered.

planting.note: Plant in cool shade in early spring.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, evenly moist soil and shelter from afternoon sun. The narrow leaves carry soft silver speckling, and the flowers open pink then settle to sky-blue in early spring. Remove faded stems and any mildewed leaves to keep the clump fresh.

planting.note: Plant in partial to full shade in early spring.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile soil and mulch to keep the roots cool. Long silver-spotted leaves back an abundant display of large cobalt-blue flowers in mid spring. Keep it watered in dry spells to preserve the foliage through summer.

planting.note: Plant in shade with rich, moist soil in early spring.

directions: Set plants in cool shade and keep the soil consistently moist. The nearly solid silver leaves have a slim green margin, giving strong season-long presence. Violet-blue flowers with a hint of red appear over a long stretch in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in partial to full shade in early spring.

directions: Plant in moist woodland soil where the plain deep-green leaves stay lush. This selection is grown for its exceptionally large, rich dark-blue flowers rather than for foliage markings. Blooms open in early spring on compact stems above the emerging leaves.

planting.note: Plant in partial to full shade in early spring.

directions: Plant in rich, evenly moist soil and avoid hot, dry sites. The broad, wavy leaves are almost entirely frosted silver and hold well through the season. Flowers open coral-pink and shift to soft lavender and blue in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in cool shade in early spring; keep soil moist for best foliage.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, moisture-retentive soil in a shaded bed. The long leaves are washed almost solidly silver with a narrow green edge, making a bright groundcover. Pink flowers age to lilac-blue in early spring; shear tired leaves in midsummer if they scorch.

planting.note: Plant in partial to full shade in early spring.

directions: Plant in cool, moist shade and mulch to hold moisture. Silver-spotted green leaves set off delicate flowers that open the palest ice-blue, nearly white, in early spring. Keep evenly watered to reduce mildew and maintain the soft luminous color.

planting.note: Plant in moist shade in early spring; divide in autumn if needed.

directions: Plant in deep, moist, humus-rich soil where the long narrow leaves can develop fully. The foliage is dark green spotted with silver, and the flowers are an intense deep blue in mid spring. Water through dry spells to keep the leaves from scorching.

planting.note: Plant in partial to full shade in early spring.

directions: Plant in rich, moist soil in shade for the best leaf contrast. The dark green leaves carry bold silver spotting, and the flowers open pink then turn blue so both colors show together in early spring. Trim spent flower stems to keep the clump neat.

planting.note: Plant in cool, moist shade in early spring.

directions: Plant in humus-rich soil kept consistently moist and out of hot sun. The upright, narrow leaves are nearly solid silvery-white, forming a striking pale clump. Pink buds open to soft blue flowers in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in partial to full shade in early spring.

directions: Plant in rich, moist, well-drained soil in shade. The long silver leaves are edged and veined in green, giving a bright, well-defined pattern that lasts into summer. Reddish-violet flowers mature to blue in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in moist shade in early spring.

directions: Plant in deep, moisture-retentive soil in a shaded position. The very long, narrow leaves are almost entirely silver with a fine green rim, arching gracefully. Deep blue flowers appear in mid spring; keep watered to prevent summer leaf scorch.

planting.note: Plant in partial to full shade in early spring.

directions: Plant in cool, moist, organic soil in shade. The leaves have broad silver centers framed by dark green margins for strong definition. Pink buds open to blue flowers in early spring above the bold foliage.

planting.note: Plant in shade in early spring; compact habit suits the front of a bed.

directions: Plant this compact selection in moist, humus-rich shade. The broad leaves are heavily washed with silver, splashed as if with milk, over a low mound. Rose-pink flowers turn blue-violet in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in full shade in early spring; the variegated leaves scorch in sun.

directions: Plant in deep, cool shade with steady moisture, since the pale leaf margins burn easily in sun or dry soil. The soft green leaves are broadly edged in creamy white, and the flowers are coral-red in early spring. Remove any all-green shoots to keep the variegation stable.

planting.note: Plant in shade in early spring; one of the earliest lungworts to flower.

directions: Plant in rich, moist woodland soil and keep it evenly watered. The plain green leaves are unspotted, and the vivid coral-red flowers open very early, often in late winter to early spring. Cut back after bloom to encourage a flush of clean foliage.

planting.note: Plant in partial to full shade in early spring.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile soil in shade and mulch lightly. The narrow leaves carry light silver spotting on a compact, upright plant. Deep cobalt-blue flowers appear freely in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in cool shade in early spring.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, moist soil out of hot sun. The wavy leaves are nearly solid silver with fine dark speckling and a thin green edge. Flowers open pink and mature to lavender-blue in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in partial to full shade in early spring.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained soil enriched with compost. The dark green leaves are densely spotted with silver, giving a starry effect. Rose-red buds open to violet-blue flowers in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in partial to full shade in early spring.

directions: Plant in cool, moist shade with rich soil. The frosted silver-green leaves make a soft background for the flowers, which open coral-pink in early spring. Keep evenly watered to hold the foliage in good condition through summer.

planting.note: Plant in dappled shade in early spring.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich soil in shade. The silver-spotted leaves set off flowers that open pink and turn a soft smoky blue in early spring. Divide congested clumps after flowering to keep them vigorous.

planting.note: Plant in partial to full shade in early spring.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile soil in shade. The silver-spotted leaves form a neat mound below the flowers, which stay a warm coral-pink rather than turning blue. Blooms appear over a long period in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in partial to full shade in early spring.

directions: Plant in cool, moist shade with rich soil. The narrow silver-spotted leaves back flowers of an unusually deep, saturated cobalt to navy blue in early spring. Keep watered to reduce mildew and preserve foliage.

planting.note: Plant in shade in early spring.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich soil in a shaded spot. The silvery leaves set off flowers that open the palest icy blue, almost white, in early spring. Mulch and keep moist for the cleanest foliage.

planting.note: Plant in partial to full shade in early spring.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained soil in shade. The narrow, upright leaves carry silver spotting on a compact plant. Golden-yellow-eyed blue flowers appear early and freely in spring.

planting.note: Plant in partial to full shade in early spring.

directions: Plant in cool, moist, organic soil in shade. Silver-spotted leaves back flowers that hold a warm rose-pink before deepening in early spring. Water in dry spells to keep foliage fresh and mildew-free.
